coredata.dc:description: Ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ in aquatic ecosystems is of great significance due to its high toxicity and ubiquity in water. Herein, using a one-step in-situ synthesis method, blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), red fluorescent InP/ZnS quantum dots (InPQDs), and MOFs (ZIF-8) integrated multicolor nano-sensor CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 was constructed for consecutive visual detection of Hg2+ and Cys. The InPQDs can act as the response unit for Hg2+ and Cys, with the limit of detection (LOD) of 8.68 and 37.96 nM, respectively. Significantly, the low detection limit combines with good specificity and accuracy of the nano-sensor meet the requirement for the safety monitoring and control of Hg2+ in drinking and environmental water. Moreover, the color recognition and processing software installed on smart phone can realize the real-time and rapid sensing of Hg2+ and Cys. A logic gate circuit was also devised, providing the possibilities for the application of the nano-sensor in the field of intelligent devices. As far as we know, this was the first example to apply InPQDs to the continuous multicolor visual detection of Hg2+ and Cys, which provided reference for the construction of environmentally-friendly dual emission fluorescent sensors for hazardous substance monitoring.

head.ce:title: Intelligent multicolor nano-sensor based on nontoxic dual fluoroprobe and MOFs for colorful consecutive detection of Hg2+ and cysteine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ in aquatic ecosystems is of great significance due to its high toxicity and ubiquity in water. Herein, using a one-step in-situ synthesis method, blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), red fluorescent InP/ZnS quantum dots (InPQDs), and MOFs (ZIF-8) integrated multicolor nano-sensor CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 was constructed for consecutive visual detection of Hg2+ and Cys. The InPQDs can act as the response unit for Hg2+ and Cys, with the limit of detection (LOD) of 8.68 and 37.96 nM, respectively. Significantly, the low detection limit combines with good specificity and accuracy of the nano-sensor meet the requirement for the safety monitoring and control of Hg2+ in drinking and environmental water. Moreover, the color recognition and processing software installed on smart phone can realize the real-time and rapid sensing of Hg2+ and Cys. A logic gate circuit was also devised, providing the possibilities for the application of the nano-sensor in the field of intelligent devices. As far as we know, this was the first example to apply InPQDs to the continuous multicolor visual detection of Hg2+ and Cys, which provided reference for the construction of environmentally-friendly dual emission fluorescent sensors for hazardous substance monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Toxic heavy metal ions, which can cause serious diseases and environmental pollution, pose a serious problem to public health and ecosystems (Dai et al., 2020; Sahin et al., 2020; Kou et al., 2020). In particular, Hg2+ as one of the most hazardous pollutant, can cause severe irreversible damage to the central nervous system and immune system even at very low concentrations (Clarkson et al., 2003). Because of the highly toxic effects of Hg2+, the World Health Organization (WHO) (Wang et al., 2020) and U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (Sahin et al., 2020) have limited the maximum level for Hg2+ in drinking water to 30 and 10 nM, respectively. Considering that the harmful Hg2+ is mostly distributed in aqueous solutions and can accumulate in organisms through the food chain, it is of great significance to design and synthesize an environmentally friendly, efficient and economical Hg2+ testing material and establish a feasible and effective Hg2+ testing method for the regular monitoring of environmental water samples.

Currently, miscellaneous technologies involving electrochemical analysis (Liu et al., 2009), atomic absorption spectroscopy (Vil'pan et al., 2005), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (Lepak et al., 2015), and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (Kang et al., 2014; Balasurya et al., 2021a, 2021b), have been utilized to monitor Hg2+. The traditional heavy metal detection usually used large-scale analysis and detection equipment, which cannot realize the rapid visual on-site detection. The above detection process was often complex, and the cost of manpower and material resources was relatively high (Wang et al., 2020). Therefore, it is extremely important to develop a simple, convenient and efficient visual analysis and detection method for heavy metals. In contrast, luminescent and colorimetric detection technology (Kokilavani et al., 2022, 2020a, 2020b, 2020c, 2020d; Alam et al., 2015; Janani et al., 2020a, 2020b, 2020c, 2020d, 2020e) has attracted more and more attention due to its advantages of simple design, low cost, good visibility and low detection limit.

To date, various luminescent materials has been constructed for Hg2+ detection, such as semiconductor quantum dot (Chen et al., 2018), gold/sliver nanoclusters (Guo et al., 2009; Xie et al., 2010), metal/covalent organic frameworks (Samanta et al., 2018; Ding et al., 2016), organic dye molecules (Xu et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2019), and lanthanide complexes (Tan et al., 2012; Li et al., 2014). However, their weaknesses of high toxicity, high cost, poor photothermal stability or low yield limit their practical use. Especially, with the growing global tendency to restrict the use of hazardous elements, environmentally-friendly fluorescent materials have attracted great attention. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) as novel luminescent materials with merits of low toxicity, low-cost, high photostability, and facile fabrication process stand out in the fields of luminescence sensing. However, the reported instances for Hg2+ detection are basically based on single response fluorescent probes (Cui et al., 2015; Lu et al., 2012; Pajewska-Szmyt et al., 2020). In comparison, Ratio fluorescence method is an analytical method to measure the target by measuring the fluorescence intensity ratio at two different wavelengths as the signal parameter (Jia et al., 2021a, 2021b; Xu et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021), in which the non-toxic ratiometric fluorescent probes are particularly attractive and are still in urgent need. Since the measured fluorescence ratio signal is not affected by the intensity of the light source and the sensitivity of the instrument, the ratio fluorescent probe has high sensitivity, selectivity and linear range.

Notably, InP/ZnS quantum dots (InPQDs) has recently emerged as a fascinating material with low toxicity, broad color tenability and large absorption coefficient, which make them promising alternatives to classic Cd/Pb-based QDs (Chen et al., 2020). However, fluorescence probe based on InPQDs has been rarely reported so far.

Herein, an intelligent multicolor nano-sensor is elaborately designed by encapsulating non-toxicity CDs and InPQDs into ZIF-8 to construct CDs/InPQDs@ ZIF-8 nanocomposite for ultrasensitive visual detection of Hg2+ and subsequent Cys ( Scheme 1). The low detection limit (8.68 nM) accompanied by good specificity and accuracy for Hg2+ can realize the monitor of Hg2+ in river, tap water, synthetic seawater and synthetic wastewater samples. Moreover, the color recognition and processing software installed on smart phone can realize the real-time and rapid sensing of Hg2+ and Cys. A logic gate circuit is also designed to expand the application of CDs/InPQDs@ ZIF-8 nano-sensor in the field of intelligent detection. This is the first time that InPQDs have been applied for the fluorescence detection of metal ions, as well as used for the preparation of MOF-based composites, which provides reference for the construction of novel environmentally-friendly dual emission fluorescent materials for hazardous substance monitoring.

Zn(NO3)2.6H2O, representative amino acids and organic solvents were purchased from Tianjin Komiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (99.0%), 2-methylimidazole was ordered from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (98.0%), InP/ZnS QDs were obtained from Suzhou Xingshuo Nano Technology Co., Ltd., kelp powder was obtained from WudiLvqi Biological Engineering Co., Ltd. HEPES was ordered from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. All the water used in the experiment was deionized water. The reagents used in this experiment are all analytical grade and without further purification.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) were measured by Tecnai-G2-F30 under 300 kV acceleration voltage. UV–vis absorption spectra were collected by Perkin Elmer Lambda 950 spectrophotometer. The powder X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) was obtained using the RigakuDmax2400 diffractometer of CuKa radiation. The infrared spectrum was carried out using a PristigeIR21 FTIR infrared spectrometer. The fluorescence spectra were determined by the Varian CARY Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer. Lifetime decay measurements were carried out with Edinburgh FS5 equipped with a cooled R928 P photomultiplier tube.

Blue fluorescent CDs are fabricated through a hydrothermal method using natural biomass kelp powder as precursor. In details, kelp powder (1 g) was dispersed in 15 mL of deionized water and ultrasound for 10 min. Then the mixture was heated at 210 °C for 10 h in a Teflon reactor. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting mixture was centrifuged twice at 8500 rpm to remove the precipitate. The supernatant was firstly filtered with a 0.22 µm filter membrane, and then was dialyzed in ultrapure water through a cellulose membrane (MWCO 500) for 2 days. The powder of CDs was obtained by lyophilization.

CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 was obtained by adding two fluorescent materials in the process of preparing ZIF-8. In detail, 2-methylimidazole (16.0 mmol), 1.7 mL of InPQDs (50 nmol/L), 120 μg CDs and 30 mL methanol were mixed and stirred for 1 h. Zn(NO3)2.6H2O (1.0 mmol) was added to the above mixed solution drop by drop and the mixture was stirred for 8 h and left to stand for 2 h. The resulting product was collected by centrifugal and vacuum-dried at 50 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ and Cys with the CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 nano-sensor
ce:section.ce:para: CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 (20 mg) was dispersed in 10 mL HEPES (0.06 mol/L pH = 8.0) buffer solution and ultrasound for 2 min. To investigate the response of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 towards Hg2+, 100 μL of the above CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 solution (2 mg/mL) was added to 1.5 mL HEPES (0.06 mol/L pH = 8.0) buffer solution, and then different concentrations (0–8.5 μM) of Hg2+ ion were added. The solution was diluted to 2 mL using HEPES buffer solution. After incubating for 3 min at room temperature, the solution was excited at 360 nm and the emission spectrum was recorded.

To study the effect of Cys on the CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+ composite, CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+ (0.2 mg/mL CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+, 5 μM Hg2+) was added to 1.5 mL of HEPES (0.06 mol/L, pH = 8.0). Titrate the above solution with Cys solutions of different concentrations (0–130 μM), and dilute to 2 mL. The mixture was incubated at room temperature for 8 min, and excited at 360 nm. The relative ratio of the fluorescence emission intensity at 620 and 440 nm was collected.

By observing the fluorescence stability of the nano-sensor to common interference sources (such as common metal ions, representative amino acids) in food, the selectivity of the nano-sensor to mercury ion was studied in detail. A similar method as described in Section 2.5 was used to introduce 10 μM interfering analyte instead of Hg2+. In addition, the fluorescence recovery effect of common amino acids on red emission of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+ was also investigated. According to the described method in Section 2.5, various amino acid solutions (50 μM) were added instead of Cys.

In order to evaluate the practical ability of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 nano-sensor for Hg2+ detection, standard addition methods were used to monitor Hg2+ in river and tap water. In addition, synthetic seawater and wastewater samples were also investigated here. River water samples were taken from Dasha River (Jiaozuo, China) and pretreated according to the method we reported previously (Jia et al., 2021b). The tap water was taken from unpurified water from local pipes. The synthetic seawater and wastewater samples with compositions as listed in Table S1 were prepared according to the reported literatures (Pytlakowska et al., 2021; de la Calle et al., 2019).

The CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 (2 mg/mL, 100 μL) nano-sensor was dispersed in 1.6 mL HEPES (0.06 mol/L, pH = 8.0) buffer, and then standard solutions containing different concentrations of Hg2+ were added to the sample (river water, tap water, synthetic seawater and wastewater), then the solution was excited at 360 nm and the fluorescence emission spectrum of the detection system was recorded.

The paper-based nanosenor used for Hg2+ determination is made from filter paper, which were immersed in the aqueous solution of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 (approximately 2 mg/mL) for 30 min. Then samples containing Hg2+ ion with certain concentration (0–7 μM) was dropped to the paper, and the fluorescent color change was detected by naked eyes under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 nano-sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 fluorescent nano-sensor with two fluorescent emission properties were prepared by adding two kinds of fluorescent nanoparticles CDs and InPQDs into the MOF skeleton forming process by a simple one-pot method. Since the fluorescence intensity of InPQDs was stronger than that of CDs, after the introduction of corresponding characteristic pollutants, the fluorescence of InPQDs changed while that of CDs remained unchanged, so the erasable multicolor fluorescence visual sensing of various pollutants can be realized. As depicted in the TEM Fig. 1a and b, both CDs and InPQDs were spherical and uniformly distributed with mean particle sizes of 1.54 and 1.97 nm, respectively. As portrayed in Fig. 1c-e, both ZIF-8 and CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 exhibited a rhombic dodecahedron structure with average diameter of about 63.44 and 61.80 nm, respectively. The EDS spectrum in Fig. 1f indicated elements of In, Zn, P, S, C, N, O were clearly observed in the as-synthesized nano-sensor. The chemical composition and elemental distribution of CDs/InPQDs@ ZIF-8 were investigated by EDX-mapping. Clearly form Fig. S1, C, N, O, Zn, P, S and In elements were distributed on CDs/InPQDs@ ZIF-8, which confirmed that the CDs and InPQDs were captured in ZIF-8.

The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of the synthesized ZIF-8 and CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S2, the staged oxidation removal process in ZIF-8 also existed in the TGA curve of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8, indicating the integrity of ZIF-8 frame structure after encapsulated with fluorescent nanoparticles (Liu et al., 2016). Moreover, CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 sample had a small amount of weight loss in the temperature range of 200–350 °C compared with ZIF-8 sample, which could be attributed to the partial loss of CDs/InPQDs (Shishodia et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2010).

The chemical bonds of CDs, InPQDs, ZIF-8 and CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 were investigated through FT-IR measurements. As delineated in Fig. S3, the absorption peaks at 3373, 2825 and 1670 cm−1 in CDs can be attributable to the stretching vibration of O-H (or/and N-H), C-H and CO, respectively (Boruah et al., 2020). The peaks at 1589 and 1413 cm−1 were due to the stretching vibration of N-H and COO-, respectively (Liang et al., 2014), while the absorption peak at 1032 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of C-O (Ahn et al., 2019). As for InPQDs, the absorption peaks at 3317 cm−1 and 1634 cm−1 were attributed to O-H and CO stretching vibration, respectively (Yang et al., 2019; Ranjbar-Navazi et al., 2021), indicating the existence of abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of InPQDs. Fig. S3a also indicated that the CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 exhibited similar FT-IR spectrum to ZIF-8. In addition, the absorption peak at 3317 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 may be attributed to the stretching vibration of -OH in InPQDs (Yang et al., 2019), while the peaks at 2825 cm−1 and 1032 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of the C-H and C-O (Boruah et al., 2020; Ahn et al., 2019) in CDs, respectively, confirming the embedding of InPQDs and CDs in the ZIF-8 framework. Besides, XRD analysis was performed to study the crystallinity and phase purity. Among the various peak positions of the simulated ZIF-8 crystal shown in Fig. S3b, the peaks of 2θ= 7.3°, 10.4° and 12.7° were corresponded to the (011), (002) and (112) fronts of typical ZIF-8 crystals, respectively. Fig. S3b indicated the characteristic diffraction peaks of ZIF-8 and CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 were in good agreement with the simulated diffraction pattern of ZIF-8, which revealed the intact rhombic dodecahedron structure of ZIF-8 framework in the final nanosensor.

Moreover, fluorescence spectra of CDs, InPQDs, ZIF-8 and CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 were investigated. As displayed in Fig. 2a, two emission bands located at 440 nm and 620 nm can be obviously observed in the fluorescence spectra of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8, which were consistent with the emission bands of CDs and InPQDs, respectively. The result clearly confirmed the fluorescence of the nano-sensor was originated from CDs and InPQDs. To verify that CDs and InPQDs were encapsulated in ZIF-8 rather than attached on its surfaces, CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 was washed with anhydrous methanol, and the fluorescence spectrum of the supernatant was collected, which showed almost no emission under the previous test conditions (Fig. 2a, Ex=360 nm). These results proved that all InPQDs and CDs were wrapped in ZIF-8, indicating the successful formation of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8. It was worth noting that the two emission peaks of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 had large Stokes shifts (440 nm and 620 nm), which not only had rich fluorescence color adjustability, but also improved the detection sensitivity of specific substrates, and were more conducive to the practical application of dual emission probes.

Compared to the obviously excitation-dependent property of CDs (Fig. S4), CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 exhibited stable emission centers at 440 and 620 nm under different excitations (Fig. 2b), indicating that the crystal structure of ZIF-8 had a significant restriction on CDs. The excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of CDs and InPQDs are portrayed in Fig. S4 and S5, respectively. The UV–vis spectra of CDs, InPQDs, ZIF-8 and CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 were also recorded as delineated in Fig. S6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of the best sensing conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the best detection performance of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 towards Hg2+ and the subsequent Cys, a series of experiments were carried out to optimize the test conditions ( Kokilavani et al., 2020a, 2020b, 2020c, 2020d; Kumar et al., 2021). As shown in Fig. S7, during the detection of Hg2+ and Cys, the fluorescence intensity ratio at 620 nm and 440 nm of the original probe was 8. Although the I620/I440 after adding Hg2+ and l probe was the largest at pH 8, indicating that the optimal working pH of the Cys had little difference at pH 8 and 10, in order to ensure the maximum initial fluorescence intensity of the probe, the subsequent experiments were carried out in the buffer of pH 8 (Kokilavani et al., 2021; Balasurya et al., 2021a, 2021b; Balasurya et al., 2020a, 2020b, 2020c, 2020d, 2020e, 2020f). As shown in Fig. S8, after adding a certain amount of Hg2+ and Cys, the I620/I440 ratio of the probe reached the maximum in 3 min, indicating that the time interval for each sample addition was 3 min. Since Cys detection was mainly based on its strong interaction with Hg2+, appropriate Hg2+ played an extremely important role in the fluorescence recovery of probes during Cys detection. Fig. S9 showed that when the concentration of Hg2+ was 5 μM, the fluorescence recovery effect of the composite nanoprobe CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+ was the best, so the amount of Hg2+ in the composite probe was constant at 5 μM in the detection of Cys. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of the probe itself was also an important indicator to evaluate whether a probe can well complete the sensitive detection of the target. The results of Fig. S10 and S11 showed that the fluorescence intensity of the probe had little change within 1 h and after 30 days, indicating that the probe had good fluorescence stability at room temperature.

Under the above optimal sensing condition, the performance of the CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 nano-sensor for Hg2+ detection was systematically investigated by fluorescence titration. As delineated in Fig. 3a, after adding different amounts of Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity of the nano-sensor at 620 nm was significantly reduced, while the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm remained basically unchanged, indicating that the nano-composited fluorescence sensor can realize the fluorescence discoloration sensing of mercury ions. As portrayed in Fig. 3b, the fluorescent intensity ratio of I440/I620 and concentrations of Hg2+ showed a good linear relationship within the concentration range of 0–5 µM (I440/I620 = 0.1686 [Hg2+] + 0.5626) with R2 = 0.9926. The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I440/I620) of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 and Hg2+ in the low concentration range (0–0.5 μM) was listed in the Fig. S12, from which we can see the nano-sensor still showed good detection performance to Hg2+ between 0 μM to 0.5 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) of the nano-sensor for Hg2+ was calculated to be 8.68 nM, which was lower than the permitted amount for Hg2+ in drinking water set by the WHO (30 nM) and U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (10 nM). Meanwhile, the LOD value here was obviously lower than that of the majority of luminescence probes (Table S2) (Yuan et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2017; Janani et al., 2020a, 2020b, 2020c, 2020d, 2020e; Balasurya et al., 2020a, 2020b, 2020c, 2020d, 2020e, 2020f; Ding et al., 2016; Lin et al., 2017; Rasheed et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2013; Samanta et al., 2018; Cui et al., 2020; Hai et al., 2018; Kokilavani et al., 2020a, 2020b, 2020c, 2020d), manifesting the high sensitivity of the nano-sensor. The fluorescence intensity variation of the nano-sensor upon the addition of Hg2+ was consistent with the chromaticity diagram of the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) (Fig. 3c), as well as the gradually color transition of the reaction system from red to blue under UV lamp at 365 nm (Fig. 3d). In comparison with the single signal responsive fluorescent probe, the multicolor fluorescent nano-sensor here can provide a built-in self-calibration and offer higher degree of visualization during Hg2+ determination.

Moreover, the quenching effect of Hg2+ on the CDs/InPQDs system was also investigated under the optimal test condition. Similar quenching law as CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 can be observed upon the addition of Hg2+ (Fig. S13a). However, the relative fluorescence intensity ratio I440/I620 of CDs/InPQDs varied linearly with the concentration of Hg2+ within two ranges of 0–2 and 2–8 μM (Fig. S13b), correspond to the LOD of 1703 and 199.05 nM, respectively. The above results manifested the excellent sensitivity of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 for Hg2+ detection compared to CDs/InPQDs system. The superior sensitivity of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 may mainly originate from the porous structure and confinement of MOF, which facilitated the generation and stabilization of the evenly dispersed CDs and InPQDs, and the large surface area provided by the porous structures of MOFs also enhanced the contact area between the nano-sensor and the analyte (Yi et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cys using CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: As an important thiol-containing amino acid, cysteine (Cys) plays vital biological roles related to the synthesis of protein, metabolism and detoxification. (Le et al., 2019). Considering the strong affinity between Hg2+ and -SH group in Cys, consecutive sensing assay for Cys was carried out based on the recovery of red fluorescence emission of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+. The reason was that the strong binding force between Cys and Hg2+ ions led to the emergence of Cys-Hg2+ complex, and the separation of Hg2+ ions from CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+ promoted the final fluorescence recovery. Fig. 4a proved that the fluorescence intensity of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+ at 620 nm gradually enhanced with the increase of Cys. In order to ensure the high sensitivity and wide linear region of the CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+ towards Cys, the amount of 5 μM Hg2+ ion was adopted to monitor Cys (Fig. S9). As portrayed in Fig. 4b, Cys (0–130 μM) was gradually added to the CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+ system, a linear relationship (I620/I440 = 0.03873[Cys] + 0.61765) can be obtained between the intensity ratio I620/I440 and the concentrations of Cys (0–7.5 μM), with the LOD of 37.96 nM.

The possible quenching mechanism of the CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 nano-sensor induced by Hg2+ was further explored. In general, the fluorescence quenching can be resulted from dynamic quenching, static quenching, or a combination of both. (Wu et al., 2020) Both the dynamic and static quenching conformed to the conventional Stern-Volmer equation (Zhai et al., 2014).
F 0/F=1+K sv[Q]
Where K sv is the Stern−Volmer quenching constant, and F 0, F are the FL intensities of the nano-sensor in the absence and presence of quencher, [Q] is the concentration of the quencher.

As depicted in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence quenching data followed the above equation, and the value of K sv was calculated to be 277.2 nM−1, demonstrating either a dynamic or a static mechanism (Lu et al., 2012). To further ascertain what kind of quenching effect was involved in FL quenching process, the lifetime of InPQDs at 620 nm in CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8, CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+ and CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+-Cys nanocomposites were determined respectively (Fig. 5b). The results revealed that the fitting of the fluorescence lifetimes conformed to the typical double exponential model: F(t)= b1exp(-t/τ1)+ b2exp(-t/τ2), and the average lifetime was calculated as τ = (b1τ1 2 + b2τ2 2)/(b1τ1 + b2τ2) (Yi et al., 2020). Fig. 5b indicated the average fluorescence lifetimes of InPQDs in CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8, CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+ (5 μM Hg2+) and CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+-Cys (100 μM Cys) were 31.31, 18.10 and 28.04 ns, respectively, indicating dynamic quenching process and obvious interactions between the nano-sensor and the analyte (Yan et al., 2019). Besides, the fluorescence lifetime of the reference FL signal of CDs was also collected. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 was 3.92 ns, and almost no change can be observed after adding Hg2+ (3.51 ns) and Cys (3.81 ns) (Fig. S14), indicating the stability of the reference FL signal of CDs. In addition, the UV–vis absorption spectra of InPQDs, Hg2+ and InPQDs-Hg2+ were also investigated as portrayed in Fig. S15. No new peak can be observed in the spectra of InPQDs-Hg2+ compared to that of Hg2+ and InPQDs, excluding the static quenching mechanism in the InPQDs-Hg2+ system, which was consistent with the above conclusion based on fluorescence lifetime.

From the above quenching experiments, FL lifetime determination and UV–vis absorption results, the FL quenching of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 might be caused by the electron transfer (ET) effect between Hg2+ and the oxygen-containing carboxy group on the surface of InPQDs, which changed the electron cloud density of InPQDs and resulted in the decrease of energy traps (Sun et al., 2011). In details, the -COOH group on the surface of InPQDs was inclined to lose its electrons forming ionized carboxylic groups (-COO−), which can intensely chelate with Hg2+ (Luo et al., 2019). Hg2+ catched the electrons of the conduction band from photoexcited InPQDs, generating Hg(COO)2 on the surface of InPQDs, which accelerated the non-radioactive recombination of excitons via effective ET, and led to the alteration of surface state, resulting in the efficient FL quenching of InPQDs (Hou et al., 2015). Furthermore, upon the addition of Cys as a strong Hg2+ chelator to the CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+ system, Hg2+ was removed from the surface of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 due to the formation of Hg−S bond with Cys, leading to the FL recovery of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 (Fig. 4a).

To evaluate the selectivity of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 towards Hg2+ ion, the effect of common metal ions (10 μM) was investigated under the optimal experimental conditions. As portrayed in Fig. 6a and b, Hg2+ ion resulted in obvious FL quenching, while Cu2+ ion led to FL quenching to a certain extent and other metal cations induced almost no intensity changes, indicating high selective towards Hg2+. To further confirm the superiority of the nano-sensor for Hg2+determination, Cu2+ was detected separately using the nano-sensor under the same test conditions. As shown in Fig. S16, although Cu2+ also had a certain response to the fluorescence sensor in a small concentration range (0–7 µM), the calculated LOD was 88.60 nM, which exhibited obviously poorer sensitivity than that of Hg2+ detection (8.68 nM).

More importantly, color transformation during the detection of Cu2+ only exhibit brightness change, which cannot realize visual multicolor testing. These results verified the prominent selectivity of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 towards Hg2+ ion visual detection. Furthermore, the selectivity of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+ towards Cys was also assessed. As depicted in Fig. 6c and d, only the introduction of Cys can lead to obvious FL enhancement of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+, manifesting excellent selectivity for Cys detection. The reason was that the strong binding force between Cys and Hg2+ ions led to the emergence of Cys-Hg2+ complex, and the separation of Hg2+ ions from CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+ promoted the final fluorescence recovery.

The above results proved that CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 nano-sensor had good selectivity and sensitivity for Hg2+. In order to expand the application potential of the nano-sensor for Hg2+ detection in practical samples, it is necessary to evaluate the anti-interference ability of the probe for common coexisting anions and cations (Balasurya et al., 2020a, 2020b, 2020c, 2020d, 2020e, 2020f; Janani et al., 2020a, 2020b, 2020c, 2020d, 2020e). Therefore, in the subsequent experiments, the effects of some coexisting cations (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Ag+, Li+, Sn2+, Cr3+ and Fe3+) and anions (S2-, ClO4 -，CH3COO-, SO4 2-, PO4 3-, NO3 -, HCO3 -, CO3 2-, F-, Cl-, Br- and I-) on the fluorescence intensity of the nano-sensor were investigated. As shown in Fig. S17, the CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 nano-sensor still showed good Hg2+ detection performance in the presence of different cations or anions, indicating that the prepared nano-sensor had good selectivity, anti-interference and sensitivity for Hg2+.

To verify the practical application of the CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 nano-sensor for Hg2+ determination, the above multicolor sensing method was also used to monitor Hg2+ in river, tap water, synthetic seawater and synthetic wastewater samples. As depicted in Table S3, the recoveries of Hg2+ in the detection process were within the range of 96.40–112.00%, while the relative standard deviation (RSD) ranges between 0.68% and 2.38% (Table S3), manifesting the feasibility of this multicolor sensing platform for practical application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual measurement of Hg2+ using test strips and intelligent detection using color scanning APP
ce:section.ce:para: Portable and low-cost test strips were constructed for visible detection of Hg2+ by immobilizing red fluorescent CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 nano-sensor on filter paper. Fig. S18 displayed that obvious color transition of the test paper (from red to purple and blue) can be observed with the naked eye accompanied by the concentration increase of Hg2+. It can also be seen from Fig. S18c that with the addition of mercury ions, the fluorescence color of the test strips changed greatly, and each concentration corresponded to almost different fluorescence colors, which was beneficial to the realization of rapid semi-quantitative and qualitative detection of mercury ions.

In addition, color picture extraction and RGB (optical primary colors, R for red, G for green, B for blue) value analysis APP on smart phones enabled quantitative analysis of fluorescent colors. This APP can analyze the relative chroma of blue and red in the fluorescence color of the test paper sensor before and after adding mercury ions to achieve accurate and intelligent detection of Hg2+. Fig. 7 illustrated excellent linearity with Y = 0.2121X + 0.8618 in a narrow concentration range (0–7 μM) between B/R ratio and Hg2+ concentrations (R2 = 0.9918). At the same time, the lowest detection concentration (LDC) of Hg2+ by the novel fluorescent sensor was about 7.22 nM, which was far lower than the maximum residual concentration (30 nM) stipulated by the World Health Organization (Wang et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the fluorescent nano-sensor in logic gate circuit
ce:section.ce:para: Since the red fluorescence of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 fluorescent nano-sensor can be effectively quenched and recovered by adding Hg2+ and Cys, respectively, the fluorescent nano-sensor can be applied to logic gate circuits ( Fig. 8a.) to achieve semi-quantitative analysis of Hg2+ and Cys.

Firstly, an advanced one-to-two logic gate decoder (Xu et al., 2017) was designed for the multicolor fluorescence change of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 nano-sensor system caused by the change of Hg2+ concentration. As shown in Fig. 8a, with the change of Hg2+ concentrations (0–8.5 μM) as input signal input 1, the input signal was defined as "1" in the presence of Hg2+ and "0" in the absence of Hg2+. Two normalized fluorescence signals at 620 nm (FRed) and 440 nm (FBlue) were used as dual-output positions to realize the fluorescence signal transformation from one input position to two output positions. In the absence of Hg2+, there was no increase or decrease of FRed and FBlue respectively, and the output is (0, 1). In the presence of Hg2+, FRed channel opened, while FBlue channel did not decrease, resulting the output (1, 0). The normalized fluorescence intensity histogram of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 nano-sensor system (threshold is 2.5 μM) and the truth table of one-to-two logic gate decoder are given in Fig. 8b and 10c, respectively, and the fluorescence color changes of CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 nano-sensor system under various input states are displayed. In order to better demonstrate the change of fluorescence signal caused by the increase of Hg2+ concentrations (0–8.5 μM), the output signals of this more advanced logic gate decoder were divided into three logic groups: NOT (0,1), PASS (0,0) and YES (1,0). The Hg2+ concentration (0–8.5 μM) was divided into 0–2.5 μM, 2.5–5 μM and 5–8.5 μM as the input signals, corresponding to the NOT, PASS and YES operations, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8c, when the input Hg2+ concentration was lower than 2.5 μM, the non-gate was triggered and the red light channel was opened to generate output signal (0, 1). When the Hg2+concentrations of 5 μM (medium and high concentrations) and 8.5 μM (high concentrations) were input, PASS (0, 0) and YES (1, 0) were triggered respectively to open the blue light channel.

Since the CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 fluorescent nano-sensor with Hg2+ can realize the subsequent detection of Cys, as shown in Fig. 8a, a logic gate device with Cys concentration variation was designed based on CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+ (5 μM) (Liao et al., 2019). Input 2 was defined as “1” when the input Hg2+ concentration is 5 μM, and “0” when other concentrations were added. Input 3 was defined as “1” in the presence of Cys (0–130 μM) and “0” in the absence of Cys. Output 2 was the red fluorescence channel at 620 nm (FRed). When the red light channel was opened, the value of Output 2 was “1”, and when the red light channel was closed, it was “0”. As shown in Fig. 8d, the input (1, 0) indicated that the CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+ system did not contain Cys, and the output of the red light channel was “0”. The input (1, 1) indicated the presence of Cys in the CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8-Hg2+ system, and the red light channel was turned on and output was “1”. The inputs (0, 1) and (0, 0) indicated that the CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 system did not contain Hg2+. At this time, the red light channel was opened and the output was “1”. In summary, this logic gate device designed according to concentration and color changes was expected to be used for semi-quantitative analysis of Hg2+ and Cys.

In short, a multicolor fluorescent nano-sensor based on nontoxic CDs, InPQDs and MOFs was constructed for consecutive visual detection of Hg2+ and Cys. CDs can be serviced as a reference signal, while InPQDs featured by a maximum emission of 620 nm can be effectively quenched and subsequently recovered upon the addition of Hg2+ and continuous Cys, respectively. The low detection limit (8.68 nM) combines with good specificity and accuracy can realize the safety monitoring and control of Hg2+ in drinking water and environmental water samples. As far as we know, this is the first example to apply InPQDs to the continuous multicolor visual detection of Hg2+ and Cys, which provides a useful reference for the construction of environmentally friendly dual emission fluorescence sensors for the monitoring of harmful substances. Notably, through the integration of RGB value analysis APP on smart phones and nano-sensor immobilized test strips, Hg2+ can be intelligently detected by the constructed visual nano-sensor. Moreover, according to the discoloration of the sensing system induced by the variation of Hg2+ concentration, a logic gate circuit was devised, providing the possibilities for the application of nano-sensor in the field of intelligent devices.

In this research, CDs/InPQDs@ZIF-8 has been constructed through a one-step in-situ synthesis method for ultrasensitive visual consecutive detection of Hg2+ and Cys. The LOD for Hg2+ and Cys is 8.68 and 37.96 nM, respectively. Moreover, the color recognition and processing software installed on smart phone can realize the real-time and rapid sensing of Hg2+ and Cys. As far as we know, this is the first time that InPQDs have been applied for the fluorescence detection of metal ions, which can provide reference for the construction of novel environmentally-friendly dual emission fluorescent materials for hazardous substance monitoring.

Lina Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing-Original draft preparation, Yiru Xu: Visualization, Investigation, Software, Jun Xu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing- Reviewing and Editing, Huiju Zhang: Formal analysis, Data curation, Tongqian Zhao: Conceptualization, Supervision, Lei Jia: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.
